_comment: NotebookLM Distiller template library. Edit this file to add/modify templates without touching distill.py.

sections.heading: Opening & Setup (科普播客开场)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the opening segment (approx. 3 min) of a popular-science podcast. Two hosts: HOST A is an enthusiastic generalist who finds this topic exciting and asks the questions a curious layperson would ask; HOST B is a knowledgeable but skeptical expert who corrects oversimplifications and is quick to say 'it's more complicated than that'. Format: HOST A: ... / HOST B: ... dialogue. This segment must include: (1) A surprising hook — one fact or claim that seems wrong at first; (2) HOST A makes an intuitive assumption about the topic; (3) HOST B immediately challenges or complicates that assumption with evidence from the sources. Do NOT let both hosts agree easily. End with an open question that sets up the rest of the episode. Cite sources inline: [Source: title].

sections.heading: Core Explainer with Debate (核心内容与分歧)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the core segment (approx. 10 min) of a popular-science podcast. Two hosts: HOST A (enthusiastic generalist, optimistic, drawn to exciting implications); HOST B (skeptical expert, focuses on caveats, failure modes, and what the evidence actually supports). Format: HOST A: ... / HOST B: ... dialogue. This segment must include: (1) 3 key ideas from the sources, introduced by HOST A in plain language; (2) For EACH idea, HOST B pushes back with a specific limitation, counterexample, or nuance — not just to be difficult, but because the evidence genuinely supports a more complex picture; (3) At least one moment where HOST B says something like 'Actually, the data shows the opposite in certain conditions...' or 'People often assume X, but the sources point to Y'; (4) HOST A may update their view in response, or hold their ground with a valid reason. Avoid empty affirmations ('Great point!', 'Exactly!'). Cite sources inline: [Source: title].

sections.heading: Critical Reflection & Takeaway (批判性反思与总结)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the closing segment (approx. 3 min) of a popular-science podcast. Two hosts: HOST A (optimistic generalist); HOST B (critical expert). Format: HOST A: ... / HOST B: ... dialogue. This segment must: (1) Have HOST B explicitly name one thing the sources DON'T answer — a genuine knowledge gap or unresolved controversy; (2) Have HOST A offer a practical takeaway or analogy for the general audience; (3) Have HOST B qualify or refine that takeaway — what conditions would make it wrong?; (4) End with the hosts having a genuinely different opinion on whether this topic is overhyped, understated, or just right — no forced agreement. Cite sources inline: [Source: title].

sections.heading: Context & Framing (背景与立场确立)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the opening segment (approx. 3 min) of a professional podcast for industry practitioners. Two hosts: ALEX is a pragmatic practitioner who has shipped things and cares about what actually works in production; JORDAN is a systems-level critical thinker who asks 'what assumptions are we making?' and 'what breaks this at scale?'. Format: ALEX: ... / JORDAN: ... dialogue. This segment must: (1) Frame the problem space precisely — what is actually being solved, and for whom; (2) ALEX states a common industry assumption or narrative about this topic; (3) JORDAN immediately interrogates that assumption — what evidence supports it, what evidence contradicts it, what is being left out of the mainstream framing?; (4) End with a sharp framing question that will drive the rest of the episode. Cite all claims: [Source: title/date]. No vague generalities — every statement should be traceable to the sources.

sections.heading: Deep Technical Debate (深度技术辩论)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the core analysis segment (approx. 15 min) of a professional podcast. Two hosts: ALEX (pragmatic practitioner, execution-focused, optimistic about near-term viability); JORDAN (critical architect, challenges assumptions, surfaces second-order effects and failure modes). Format: ALEX: ... / JORDAN: ... dialogue. This segment must: (1) Cover 3-4 major technical or business-model dimensions from the sources; (2) For EACH dimension, ALEX presents the dominant/optimistic view with supporting data; (3) JORDAN then provides a genuine counterargument — not strawman, but a well-sourced alternative interpretation or a condition under which the mainstream view fails; (4) Include at least 2 moments of real intellectual tension where the hosts hold different positions and neither backs down without a reason; (5) At least once, JORDAN should say something like 'The source says X, but if you read it carefully, it also implies Y, which most people ignore'; (6) At least once, ALEX should acknowledge a limitation but argue why it does not change the practical conclusion. Cite all claims: [Source: title/date]. Flag information gaps explicitly when the sources are insufficient.

sections.heading: Stress-Test & Practitioner Synthesis (压力测试与实践总结)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the closing segment (approx. 4 min) of a professional podcast. Two hosts: ALEX (practitioner, action-oriented); JORDAN (critical thinker, risk-aware). Format: ALEX: ... / JORDAN: ... dialogue. This segment must: (1) Have JORDAN explicitly stress-test the most optimistic conclusion from the previous section — 'What would have to be true for this to fail badly?'; (2) Have ALEX respond with what safeguards, conditions, or signals a practitioner should watch for; (3) Name 1-2 open questions the sources cannot answer — genuine unknowns that practitioners need to watch; (4) End with role-specific recommendations: what should a practitioner do THIS QUARTER based on what the sources actually support — not generic advice; (5) The hosts should close with a genuine disagreement on the level of urgency or risk — one sees this as more urgent, the other sees more reasons to wait. No forced resolution. Cite all claims: [Source: title/date].

sections.heading: Opening Slides (Title, Agenda, Background)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, produce the opening slides of a presentation deck. Include: (1) Title slide with topic and subtitle; (2) Agenda slide listing the talk structure; (3) Problem/Background slide explaining why this topic matters. Each slide should have a title and up to 5 bullet points. Suggest a chart or diagram where relevant. Annotate data sources at the bottom.

sections.heading: Core Insight Slides
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, produce the core insight slides of a presentation (slides 4-9). Cover 3-5 major findings or insights with supporting evidence. Each slide should have a title and up to 5 bullet points. Suggest charts or diagrams where relevant. Annotate data sources at the bottom of each slide. Avoid dense paragraphs — use bullet points throughout.

sections.heading: Closing Slides (Challenges, Summary, Q&A)
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, produce the closing slides of a presentation. Include: (1) Challenges and limitations slide; (2) Solutions or recommendations slide; (3) Key takeaways summary (3 points max); (4) Call to action; (5) Q&A slide. Each slide should have a title and up to 5 bullet points. Annotate data sources at the bottom.

sections.heading: Executive Brief
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write a one-page executive brief for a senior decision-maker. Structure as markdown sections: (1) Executive Summary — 3 lines: what it is, why it matters, recommended action; (2) Key Findings — 3-4 bullet points, each with data + implication; (3) Core Metrics — the 3 most important numbers; (4) Action Recommendations — 1-2 concrete next steps; (5) Risk Flags — main risks and mitigations; (6) Reference — note the source notebook name. Constraints: total length under 300 words; conclusions first; no jargon without explanation. Cite all key claims.

sections.heading: Learning Notes
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, create structured learning notes for personal review. Structure: (1) Content overview — source, core topic, target audience; (2) Core concepts — concept name, definition, personal understanding; (3) Key insights — most surprising or counter-intuitive points, connections to prior knowledge; (4) Practical applications — where and how to apply this; (5) Open questions — unclear areas and next-study items; (6) Action items — checkboxes with concrete tasks for this week. Style: personal, practical. Paraphrase in your own words; use analogies and examples.

sections.heading: Action Plan
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, produce an actionable project plan. Structure: (1) Objective statement — specific and measurable; (2) Task breakdown — markdown table with columns: #, Task, Owner, Deadline, Dependencies; (3) Key milestones — M1, M2, M3 with target dates; (4) Risks and mitigations — table with: Risk, Impact, Mitigation; (5) Success criteria — checkboxes; (6) Resource requirements — people, budget, tools. Constraints: each task completable within one week; use concrete verbs (avoid 'enhance' or 'promote').

sections.heading: Executive Summary & Background
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the opening sections of a deep-dive analysis report. Include: (1) Executive Summary — key conclusions, most important data points, and top recommendations in a concise paragraph; (2) Background — problem definition, domain context, and research methodology or approach used by the sources. Every claim must be supported by data or citation. Clearly distinguish facts from opinions.

sections.heading: Core Analysis
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the core analysis section of a deep-dive report. Present 3 major findings, each with detailed argumentation and supporting evidence. For each finding: state the finding clearly, provide the evidence, explain why it matters, and note any caveats. Strictly cite all sources. Flag uncertain areas explicitly.

sections.heading: Comparative Analysis & Deep Insights
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the comparative analysis and insights sections. Include: (1) Comparative Analysis — how this work compares to related work or alternative approaches, strengths and weaknesses; (2) Deep Insights — root causes behind the findings, potential downstream impact, and future trends. Clearly distinguish facts from opinions. Cite all sources.

sections.heading: Recommendations & Limitations
sections.prompt: Based on the sources in this notebook, write the closing sections of a deep-dive report. Include: (1) Recommendations — role-specific actionable advice (e.g. for practitioners, for decision-makers, for researchers); (2) Limitations — gaps in this analysis, assumptions made, areas where evidence is insufficient; (3) Next Steps — open questions worth further investigation. Be concrete and honest about what is not known.
